== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>

In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.

The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>

'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>
== External Links == * [https://www.tsuruga.or.jp/ [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] Official Website] – (Official website of Tsuruga City) * [https://www.tsurugacity.jp/soshiki/14/1373.html {{ill|Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja|敦賀市の文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] – (Cultural Properties of Tsuruga City)

His legend overlaps with that of {{ill|Ameno Hiboko|ja|天之日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameno Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. }}
'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>
== External Links == * [https://www.tsuruga.or.jp/ [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] Official Website] – (Official website of Tsuruga City) * [https://www.tsurugacity.jp/soshiki/14/1373.html {{ill|Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja|敦賀市の文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] – (Cultural Properties of Tsuruga City)

== External Links == * [https://www.tsuruga.or.jp/ [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] Official Website] * [https://www.tsurugacity.jp/soshiki/14/1373.html {{ill|Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja|敦賀市の文化財|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

His legend overlaps with that of {{ill|Ameno Hiboko|ja|天之日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameno Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. }}
'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>
== External Links == * [https://www.tsuruga.or.jp/ [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] Official Website] – (Official website of Tsuruga City) * [https://www.tsurugacity.jp/soshiki/14/1373.html {{ill|Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja|敦賀市の文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] – (Cultural Properties of Tsuruga City)
== References ==
{{reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * {{Cite book |和書 |editor=福井県 |title=福井県史 |volume=通史編1（原始・古代） |publisher=福井県 |date=1993-03 |isbn=4-938772-02-7 |ref=harv}} ** {{Cite book |和書 |chapter=3-2-2 渡来文化と地域間交流 |chapter-url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T1/2a3-02-02-02-01.htm |ref={{Harvid|福井県|1993a}}}} ** {{Cite book |和書 |chapter=3-3-2 渡来の神々 |chapter-url=https://www.library-archives.pref.fukui.lg.jp/fukui/07/kenshi/T1/2a3-02-03-02-02.htm |ref={{Harvid|福井県|1993b}}}} * {{Cite book |和書 |author=坂本太郎、平野邦雄 監修 |title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 |edition=普及版 |publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}} |date=2010-11 |isbn=978-4642014588 |chapter=都怒我阿羅斯等 |ref={{Harvid|坂本|平野|2010}}}}
== External Links == * [https://www.tsuruga.or.jp/ [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] Official Website] * [https://www.tsurugacity.jp/soshiki/14/1373.html {{ill|Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja|敦賀市の文化財|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

His legend overlaps with that of {{ill|Ameno Hiboko|ja|天之日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameno Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. }}
'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

His legend overlaps with that of {{ill|Ameno Hiboko|ja|天之日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameno Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. }}
'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

His legend overlaps with that of {{ill|Ameno Hiboko|ja|天之日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameno Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. }}
'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>

His legend overlaps with that of {{ill|Ameno Hiboko|ja|天之日槍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameno Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. }}
'''都怒我阿羅斯等''' (つぬがあらしと, birth and death dates unknown) is a figure recorded in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. He is described as an ancient Korean individual and is said to be the son of the king of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
In the ''Nihon Shoki'', his name is rendered as "都怒我阿羅斯等", while other texts offer variations such as "都怒賀阿羅斯止", "都怒何阿羅斯志止", and "都奴加阿羅斯志等". The Nihon Shoki also provides his alias as "'''于斯岐阿利叱智干岐''' (うしきありしちかんき)".
He was a prince of the {{ill|Gaya Kingdom|ja|加羅国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaya Kingdom|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (also known as Daigaya/Daikara, a country in what is now southern Korea) and is said to be the eponymous figure from whom the place name [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] (Tsuruga) derives.
== Records == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', two traditions are recorded in a marginal note in the entry for the 2nd year of the reign of [[:en:Emperor Suinin|Emperor Suinin]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
The first tradition states that during the era of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]], Tsunuga Arashito—described as having horns growing from his forehead—arrived by ship from {{ill|Anamune|ja|穴門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anamune|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in [[:en:Nagato Province|Nagato Province]], via [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]], to {{ill|Kusei-ura|ja|笥飯浦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusei-ura|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> This is said to be the origin of the term "角鹿" (horned deer), which later evolved into "敦賀".
The second tradition relates that when Arashito was in his own country, a white stone obtained as compensation for a yellow ox transformed into a beautiful maiden. Arashito then sought to unite with her (a practice referred to as "まぐわい"), but the maiden left his presence and went to Japan, where she became the deity of the Himiyoso-sha at Nanba and in the former district of Toyokuni in [[:en:Toyo Province|Toyokuni]].<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref> Related traditions reminiscent of this account are also found in the legend of the "Ameno Hiboko" (天之日槍) and the tale of {{ill|Agaryū Hime no Kami|ja|阿加流比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agaryū Hime no Kami|1=Akaruhime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q555212}}, as well as in excerpts from the ''Settsu Fudoki'' cited in the ''Man'yōshū'' commentaries.<ref name="谷川1999">{{Cite book |和書 |author=谷川健一 |author-link=谷川健一 |title=日本の神々 |publisher=[[岩波書店]] |series=[[岩波新書]] 618 |date=1999-06 |isbn=4-00-430618-3 |pages=180-186}}</ref>
== Descendant Clans == In the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]]'', the following clans are listed as his descendants:<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> * Sakyō Shobana Ōichikubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arasudome. * Sakyō Shobana Shimizu-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashishi. * Yamato Province Shobana Hikata-kubi – said to be descended from Tsunuga Arashitome.
== Criticism == [[File:Himekoso-jinja, haiden-1.jpg|thumb|{{center|[[Himekoso-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] Regarding the name "Tsunuga," one theory posits that it derives from the Japanese reading of the highest Silla official Rank, "Shubuhan" (舒弗邯, also known as Kakuhan), and that "Tsunuga" later evolved into the place name "Tsuruga."<ref name="上田2012">{{Cite book |和書 |author=上田正昭 |author-link=上田正昭 |title=私の日本古代史 |volume=上（天皇とは何ものかー縄文から倭の五王まで） |publisher=[[新潮社]] |series=[[新潮選書]] |date=2012-12 |isbn=978-4-10-603720-7 |page=298}}</ref> Furthermore, "Arashito" appears to be derived from the Korean word for a wise person, as exemplified by the term "Arachichi" (閼智), which in the context of Silla and Gaya served as an honorific for nobles.<ref>『国史大辞典』敦賀郡項。</ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}} In Tsuruga, there are also Shikinaisha such as {{ill|Shirojō Jinja|ja|白城神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirojō Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja|信露貴彦神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinro Takahiko Jinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are of Silla (Shiro/ Shinro) origin.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993b}}</ref> Taken together with the name and legend of Tsunuga Arashito, these suggest that successive waves of immigrants from southern Korea settled in the Tsuruga area.<ref>{{Sfn|福井県|1993a}}</ref> However, due to similarities in time period and content, there is also a theory that Tsunuga Arashito is identical with {{ill|Sona Hakchichi|ja|蘇那曷叱知|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sona Hakchichi|1=Sonagaljilji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223410}}, an emissary from Mimana.<ref>{{Sfn|坂本|平野|2010}}</ref>
== Legends == [[File:Tsunuga-jinja (Kehi-jingu).JPG|thumb|{{center|[[Shinuga-jinja]] (Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture)}}]] According to the tradition of [[:en:Kehi Shrine|Kihiko Jinja]] in Tsuruga, Tsunuga Arashito was entrusted with the governance of Tsuruga.<ref name="氣比神宮">氣比神宮由緒書。</ref>
In front of JR [[:en:Tsuruga Station|Tsuruga Station]] stands a statue of Tsunuga Arashito.<ref>{{Cite web|和書|title=県道敦賀港線 ‐28‐|url=http://www2.pref.fukui.jp/press/atfiles/pa7e1174545936c9.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=福井県}}</ref>
== External Links == * [https://www.tsuruga.or.jp/ [[:en:Tsuruga, Fukui|Tsuruga City]] Official Website] – (Official website of Tsuruga City) * [https://www.tsurugacity.jp/soshiki/14/1373.html {{ill|Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja|敦賀市の文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuruga City Cultural Properties|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] – (Cultural Properties of Tsuruga City)
